name: pep249abc

metadata.name: pep249abc
metadata.summary: Abstract implementation of the DB 2.0 API outlined in PEP-249.

documentation:
# An Abstract PEP-249 Implementation

[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)

This library contains an abstract implementation of [PEP-249](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249)
which should make it easier to implement new databases in Python. These abstract
implementations are fully typed and the docstrings contain some (plagiarised) information
from the standard. There is a basic async implementation which could probably be improved
upon.

To demonstrate usage of this library, I'm putting together a wrapper on top of the 
excellent [DuckDB](https://duckdb.org/) library which adds full typing, context managers, 
distinct cursors, and easier to manage error types. [This wrapper is available here](https://github.com/thesketh/pyduckdb),
and is currently a bit rough around the edges. It contains a naive async implementation
which essentially just wraps every call with [`asyncio.to_thread`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.to_thread).

Tested in Python 3.7, but not extensively. This library has not been condoned by the PEP
authors, who might hate it. 

## Why would I use this?

If you're looking to implement a DB-API 2.0 interface for a database, this library
should make it easier to do so. Inheriting from the appropriate abstract base classes
will ensure that classes can't be instantiated without fully implementing the required 
behaviour. Implementations will be fully typed, and some functionality is provided 
'for free' (e.g. context managers, cursor iteration).

## Installation

```bash
python3 -mpip install pep249abc
```

## Usage

This library's base classes define a protocol which requires implementation. If you
inherit from these classes, your editor should whinge at you until you've implemented
the rest of the specification (or if you deviate from it).

```python
import pep249


class Cursor(pep249.Cursor):
    ...


class Connection(pep249.Connection):
    ...

```

To use the mixin types (e.g. to implement extensions, or to implement `execute*()` for 
the `Connection`), use multiple inheritance:

```python
import pep249

class Connection(
   pep249.CursorExecuteMixin, pep249.ConcreteErrorMixin, pep249.Connection
):
    ...

```

The async implementation is contained in a separate subpackage:

```python
from pep249 import aiopep249

class AsyncConnection(
   aiopep249.AsyncCursorExecuteMixin, 
   aiopep249.ConcreteErrorMixin,
   aiopep249.AsyncConnection,
):
    ...

```

## What has been implemented?

All of the core functionality, some 'common but slightly non-compliant' stuff (e.g. 
`TransactionalCursor`, a `Cursor` with transaction support), and some select extensions:

 - A mixin to [add the error types to the `Connection`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#connection-error).
 - A mixin to [add a reference to the `Connection` to the `Cursor`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#id28).
 - A mixin to [add support for next](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#next) 
   and [iteration](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#iter).

There is now a very basic async implementation, which was made by copy and pasting the synchronous
implementation and sprinking the words `async` and `await` in some appropriate (and likely some 
inappropriate) places.

## What has not been implemented?

Most of the optional extensions.

 - [`Cursor.rownumber`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#rownumber).
 - [`Cursor.scroll()`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#scroll).
 - [`Cursor.messages`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#cursor-messages).
 - [`Connection.messages`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#connection-messages).
 - [`Cursor.lastrowid`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#lastrowid).
 - [`Cursor/Connection.errorhandler`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#optional-error-handling-extensions).
 - [Two phase commit extensions](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#optional-two-phase-commit-extensions). 

Most of these are missing simply because I haven't encountered them in the wild and they
weren't supported by the reference implementation I had in mind. If you'd like to see
these implemented, please raise an issue! If you have examples of the features in use,
that would be helpful.

## What could be improved?

Probably a lot! In particular, I'm not happy with `Cursor.description` or the type 
constructors. The spec is quite opaque on these and I haven't done much research on how
these work or how they should tie in.

## What are the future plans?

General improvements, some further testing, some documentation, and maybe a more inspired
async implementation which more commonly mirrors [aiosqlite](https://pypi.org/project/aiosqlite/).

modules.name: pep249.cursor
documentation:
An abstract database cursor implementation, conformant with PEP 249.

Because it is common for database connections to implement the execute
functionality of the cursor, returning a cursor containing the results,
this is implemented using a set of mixins:

 - CursorExecuteMixin
 - CursorFechMixin
 - CursorSetSizeMixin

classes.name: pep249.cursor.CursorExecuteMixin

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorExecuteMixin.execute
documentation:
Execute an SQL query. Values may be bound by passing parameters
as outlined in PEP 249.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorExecuteMixin.executemany
documentation:
Execute an SQL query, parameterising the query with sequences
or mappings passed as parameters.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorExecuteMixin.callproc
documentation:
Execute an SQL stored procedure, passing the sequence of parameters.
The parameters should contain one entry for each procedure argument.

The result of the call is returned as a modified copy of the input
parameters. The procedure may also provide a result set, which
can be made available through the standard fetch methods.

The execute portions of a PEP 249 compliant Cursor protocol.

This could also be used to implement 'execute' support within the
database connection, as SQLite does.

classes.name: pep249.cursor.CursorSetSizeMixin

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorSetSizeMixin.setinputsizes
documentation:
Can be used before a call to `execute` to predefine memory areas
for the operation's parameters.

`sizes` is a sequence containing an item - a type, or an integer
specifying the maximum length for a string - for each input
parameter. If the item is None, no memory will be reserved for
that column.

Implementations are free to have this method do nothing.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorSetSizeMixin.setoutputsize
documentation:
Can be used before a call to `execute` to predefine buffer
sizes for fetches of 'large' columns (e.g. LONG, BLOB, etc.).

`size` is an int, referring to the size of the column.

`column` is an optional int, referring to the index in the
result sequence. If this is not provided, this will set
the default size for all 'large' columns in the cursor.

Implementations are free to have this method do nothing.
An implementation of size setting for cursor.

classes.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.description
documentation:
A read-only attribute returning a sequence containing a description
(a seven-item sequence) for each column in the result set.

The values returned for each column are outlined in the PEP:
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#description

If there is no result set, return None.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.rowcount
documentation:
A read-only attribute returning the number of rows that the last
execute call returned (for e.g. SELECT calls) or affected (for e.g.
UPDATE/INSERT calls).

If no execute has been performed or the rowcount cannot be determined,
this should return -1.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.arraysize
documentation:
An attribute specifying the number of rows to fetch at a time with
`fetchmany`.

Defaults to 1, meaning fetch a single row at a time.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.fetchone
documentation:
Fetch the next row from the query result set as a sequence of Python
types (or return None when no more rows are available).

If the previous call to `execute` did not produce a result set, an
error can be raised.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.fetchmany
documentation:
Fetch the next `size` rows from the query result set as a list
of sequences of Python types.

If the size parameter is not supplied, the arraysize property will
be used instead.

If rows in the result set have been exhausted, an an empty list
will be returned. If the previous call to `execute` did not
produce a result set, an error can be raised.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.fetchall
documentation:
Fetch the remaining rows from the query result set as a list of
sequences of Python types.

If rows in the result set have been exhausted, an an empty list
will be returned. If the previous call to `execute` did not
produce a result set, an error can be raised.

methods.name: pep249.cursor.CursorFetchMixin.nextset
documentation:
Skip the cursor to the next available result set, discarding
rows from the current set. If there are no more sets, return
None, otherwise return True.

This method is optional, as not all databases implement multiple
result sets.
The fetch portions of a PEP 249 compliant Cursor protocol.

classes.name: pep249.cursor.BaseCursor
documentation: A Cursor without an associated context.

classes.name: pep249.cursor.Cursor
documentation: A PEP 249 compliant Cursor protocol.

classes.name: pep249.cursor.TransactionalCursor
documentation:
A slightly non-compliant Cursor for a database which implements
transactions on a per-cursor level.

modules.name: pep249.transactions
documentation:
Transaction support (and non-support!) for PEP 249 compliant database
API implementations. These mixins effectively provide the 'context'
component of a database connection or cursor.

These abstract mixin classes provide a context manager and a finaliser
which closes the database connection.

There are three implementations:
 - A transaction free context, which is intended for use in compliant
   database cursors.
 - A transaction context, which is intended for use in compliant database
   connections (and could also be used to implement transaction support
   for cursors).
 - A dummy transaction context, which is intended for use in compliant
   database connections for databases which do not support transactions.

classes.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionFreeContextMixin

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionFreeContextMixin.close
documentation:
Close the connection.

If there are un-commited changes, this should perform a rollback.

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionFreeContextMixin.__enter__
documentation:
Allow the transaction-supporting object to be used as a context
manager.

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionFreeContextMixin.__exit__
documentation: Close the object upon exiting the context manager.

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionFreeContextMixin.__del__
documentation: Close the object if it is garbage collected.

A PEP 249 compliant 'transaction' protocol which does not implement
transactions. In the standard, this protocol would be implemented by a
database Cursor.

Classes which implement this protocol will need to implement a `close`
method, and will gain a context manager implementation.

For a transaction-free context for use with connections, see
`DummyTransactionContextMixin`.
pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods

classes.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionContextMixin

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionContextMixin.commit
documentation: Commit changes made since the start of the pending transaction.

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionContextMixin.rollback
documentation:
Roll back to the start of the pending transaction, discarding
changes.

methods.name: pep249.transactions.TransactionContextMixin.__exit__
documentation:
Close the object upon exiting the context manager.

If no exceptions were raised: try to commit the changes, rolling
back if there is an issue with the commit, and close the connection.

If an exception was raised: roll back, close the connection, re-raise
the exception (by returning True).

A PEP 249 compliant transaction protocol which implements implicitly-
started transactions. In the standard, this protocol would be
implemented by a database connection.

Classes which implement this protocol will need to implement a `close`
method, a `rollback` method, and a `commit method`; they will gain a
context manager implementation which rolls back in event of an error.

classes.name: pep249.transactions.DummyTransactionContextMixin

methods.name: pep249.transactions.DummyTransactionContextMixin.commit
documentation:
A dummy implementation of commit, for databases without transaction
support.

methods.name: pep249.transactions.DummyTransactionContextMixin.rollback
documentation:
A dummy implementation of rollback, for databases without transaction
support.

A PEP 249 compliant 'transaction' protocol which does not implement
transactions, but pretends to. `commit` and `rollback` are both no-op
functions. In the standard, this protocol would be implemented by a
database connection without transaction support.

Classes which implement this protocol will need to implement a `close`
method, and will gain a context manager implementation.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.exceptions
documentation: Alias of ..exceptions.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions
documentation:
Async transaction support (and non-support!) for PEP 249 compliant
async database API implementations. These mixins effectively provide
the 'context' component of an async database connection or cursor.

These abstract mixin classes provide an async context manager.

There are three implementations:
 - A transaction free context, which is intended for use in compliant
   async database cursors.
 - A transaction context, which is intended for use in compliant async
   database connections (and could also be used to implement transaction
   support for async cursors).
 - A dummy transaction context, which is intended for use in compliant
   async database connections for databases which do not support
   transactions.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionFreeContextMixin

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionFreeContextMixin.close
documentation:
Close the connection.

If there are un-commited changes, this should perform a rollback.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionFreeContextMixin.__aenter__
documentation:
Allow the transaction-supporting object to be used as an async context
manager.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionFreeContextMixin.__aexit__
documentation: Close the object upon exiting the context manager.

A PEP 249 compliant 'transaction' protocol which does not implement
transactions. In the standard, this protocol would be implemented by a
database Cursor.

Classes which implement this protocol will need to implement a `close`
method, and will gain a context manager implementation.

For a transaction-free context for use with connections, see
`DummyTransactionContextMixin`.
pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionContextMixin

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionContextMixin.commit
documentation: Commit changes made since the start of the pending transaction.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionContextMixin.rollback
documentation:
Roll back to the start of the pending transaction, discarding
changes.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncTransactionContextMixin.__aexit__
documentation:
Close the object upon exiting the async context manager.

If no exceptions were raised: try to commit the changes, rolling
back if there is an issue with the commit, and close the connection.

If an exception was raised: roll back, close the connection, re-raise
the exception (by returning True).

A PEP 249 compliant async transaction protocol which implements
implicitly-started transactions. In the standard, this protocol would
be implemented by an async database connection.

Classes which implement this protocol will need to implement an async
`close` method, an async `rollback` method, and an async `commit method`
and they will gain an async context manager implementation which rolls
back in event of an error.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncDummyTransactionContextMixin

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncDummyTransactionContextMixin.commit
documentation:
A dummy implementation of commit, for databases without transaction
support.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.transactions.AsyncDummyTransactionContextMixin.rollback
documentation:
A dummy implementation of rollback, for databases without transaction
support.

A PEP 249 compliant async 'transaction' protocol which does not implement
transactions, but pretends to. `commit` and `rollback` are both no-op async
functions. In the standard, this protocol would be implemented by an async
database connection without transaction support.

Classes which implement this protocol will need to implement an async `close`
method, and will gain an async context manager implementation.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor
documentation:
An abstract database cursor implementation, conformant with PEP 249.

Because it is common for database connections to implement the execute
functionality of the cursor, returning a cursor containing the results,
this is implemented using a set of mixins:

 - AsyncCursorExecuteMixin
 - AsyncCursorFechMixin
 - CursorSetSizeMixin

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.AsyncCursorExecuteMixin.execute
documentation:
Execute an SQL query. Values may be bound by passing parameters
as outlined in PEP 249.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.AsyncCursorExecuteMixin.executemany
documentation:
Execute an SQL query, parameterising the query with sequences
or mappings passed as parameters.

documentation:
Execute an SQL stored procedure, passing the sequence of parameters.
The parameters should contain one entry for each procedure argument.

The result of the call is returned as a modified copy of the input
parameters. The procedure may also provide a result set, which
can be made available through the standard fetch methods.

The execute portions of a PEP 249 compliant Cursor protocol.

This could also be used to implement 'execute' support within the
database connection, as SQLite does.

documentation:
A read-only attribute returning a sequence containing a description

The values returned for each column are outlined in the PEP:

If there is no result set, return None.

UPDATE/INSERT calls).

If no execute has been performed or the rowcount cannot be determined,

documentation:
An attribute specifying the number of rows to fetch at a time with
`fetchmany`.

Defaults to 1, meaning fetch a single row at a time.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.AsyncCursorFetchMixin.fetchone
documentation:
Fetch the next row from the query result set as a sequence of Python
types (or return None when no more rows are available).

If the previous call to `execute` did not produce a result set, an
error can be raised.

documentation:
Fetch the next `size` rows from the query result set as a list
of sequences of Python types.

If the size parameter is not supplied, the arraysize property will
be used instead.

If rows in the result set have been exhausted, an an empty list
will be returned. If the previous call to `execute` did not
produce a result set, an error can be raised.

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.AsyncCursorFetchMixin.fetchall
documentation:
Fetch the remaining rows from the query result set as a list of
sequences of Python types.

If rows in the result set have been exhausted, an an empty list
will be returned. If the previous call to `execute` did not
produce a result set, an error can be raised.

documentation:
Skip the cursor to the next available result set, discarding
rows from the current set. If there are no more sets, return
None, otherwise return True.

This method is optional, as not all databases implement multiple
result sets.
The fetch portions of a PEP 249 compliant Cursor protocol.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.BaseAsyncCursor
documentation: A Cursor without an associated context.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.AsyncCursor
documentation: A PEP 249 compliant Cursor protocol.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.cursor.TransactionalAsyncCursor
documentation:
A slightly non-compliant Cursor for a database which implements
transactions on a per-cursor level.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.types
documentation: Alias of ..types.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.type_constructors
documentation: Alias of ..type_constructors.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249
documentation:
Abstract implementation of PEP 249 using Python's async/await
implementation.

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.extensions
documentation:
Async implementations of optional extensions to the DB-API 2.0, as
outlined in PEP 249.

https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#optional-db-api-extensions

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.extensions.IterableAsyncCursorMixin
documentation:
An naive implementation of an optional extension to PEP 249 which
turns the cursor into an asynchronous iterator/iterable.

This enables code like:

`` `python
\>>> async def async_execute(cursor: AsyncCursor):
...     cursor.execute(SQL_QUERY)
...     # An alternative to cursor.fetchone()
...     value = await cursor.__anext__()
...     # Iterate over the values
...     async for value in cursor:
...         do_thing_with(value)
` ``

modules.name: pep249.aiopep249.connection
documentation:
An abstract asynchronous database connection implementation, conformant
with PEP 249.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.connection.BaseAsyncConnection

methods.name: pep249.aiopep249.connection.BaseAsyncConnection.cursor
documentation: Return an asynchronous database cursor.
An Async Connection without an associated context.
pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.connection.AsyncConnection
documentation: A PEP 249 compliant Connection protocol.

classes.name: pep249.aiopep249.connection.TransactionlessAsyncConnection
documentation:
A PEP 249 compliant Connection protocol for databases without
transaction support.

modules.name: pep249.exceptions
documentation: Exceptions outlined in PEP 249.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.Error
documentation: Base error outlined in PEP 249.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.InterfaceError
documentation:
Interface error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised for errors with the database interface.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.DatabaseError
documentation:
Database error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised for errors with the database.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.DataError
documentation:
Data error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised for errors that are due to problems with processed data.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.OperationalError
documentation:
Operational error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised for errors in the database's operation.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.IntegrityError
documentation:
Integrity error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised when errors occur which affect the relational integrity of
the database (e.g. constraint violations).

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.InternalError
documentation:
Integrity error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised when the database encounters an internal error.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.ProgrammingError
documentation:
Programming error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised for SQL programming errors.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.NotSupportedError
documentation:
Not supported error outlined in PEP 249.

Raised when an unsupported operation is attempted.

classes.name: pep249.exceptions.ConcreteErrorMixin
documentation: A concrete implementation of the Connection error mixin.

modules.name: pep249
documentation:
An abstract implementation of the DB-2.0 API, as outlined in PEP-249.

This package contains abstract base classes which should help with
building conformant database APIs. Inheriting from these base classes
will enforce implementation of the relevant functions, and will implement
some functionality (e.g. context managers) for free.

modules.name: pep249.type_constructors
documentation:
Example type constructors as specified by PEP 249, returning the
appropriate Python types. For a concrete implementation, these might
return something more database specific.

functions.name: pep249.type_constructors.TimeFromTicks
documentation: Return the time, given a Unix timestamp.

modules.name: pep249.connection
documentation: An abstract database connection implementation, conformant with PEP 249.

classes.name: pep249.connection.BaseConnection

methods.name: pep249.connection.BaseConnection.cursor
documentation: Return a database cursor.
A Connection without an associated context.

classes.name: pep249.connection.Connection
documentation: A PEP 249 compliant Connection protocol.

classes.name: pep249.connection.TransactionlessConnection
documentation:
A PEP 249 compliant Connection protocol for databases without
transaction support.

modules.name: pep249.types
documentation: Type aliases useful for the abstract DB API implementations.

modules.name: pep249.extensions
documentation:
Optional extensions to the DB-API 2.0, as outlined in PEP 249.

https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#optional-db-api-extensions

classes.name: pep249.extensions.ConnectionErrorsMixin
documentation:
An optional extension to PEP 249, providing access to mandated
exception types as members of the Connection class.

classes.name: pep249.extensions.CursorConnectionMixin

methods.name: pep249.extensions.CursorConnectionMixin.connection
documentation: The parent Connection of the implementing cursor.

An optional extension of PEP 249 which attaches a read only
reference to the Connection object the cursor was created from.

This could be implemented using `weakref`, to prevent errors if
the connection is garbage collected before the cursor.
pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods

classes.name: pep249.extensions.IterableCursorMixin
documentation:
An naive implementation of an optional extension to PEP 249 which
turns the cursor into an iterator/iterable.

This enables code like:

`` `python
\>>> cursor.execute(SQL_QUERY)
\>>> # An alternative to cursor.fetchone()
\>>> value = next(cursor)
\>>> # Iterate over the values
\>>> for value in cursor:
...    do_thing_with(value)
` ``
